content.text: CSS values are numbers, strings, keywords, or functions — and when numbers describe lengths, they need a unit. Choosing the right unit determines whether a layout scales gracefully with user preferences, viewport size, and content length. This document covers every category of CSS unit and the color and math functions that work alongside them.

attrs.alt: CSS Units Reference Map — absolute, font-relative, viewport, math functions, color values

content.text: Absolute units map to a fixed physical size. In practice, only px matters on screen — the others are intended for print stylesheets.
Screen layouts — the primary absolute unit

On a high-DPI (Retina) display, one CSS px may correspond to two or more physical screen pixels. This is handled by the browser and OS — CSS authors never need to account for device pixel ratios in px values.
px is appropriate for borders, shadows, and small fixed-size icons
Avoid px for font-size — it prevents users from adjusting text size in their browser settings
Avoid px for layout widths — relative or constraint-based sizing scales better
Font-relative units scale based on font metrics. They are the preferred choice for typography and spacing that should feel proportional to the text size.

rem is relative to the font-size of the root element (:root or <html>). The default root font-size is 16px in all browsers, meaning 1rem = 16px by default. rem is predictable because it never compounds:

:root { font-size: 16px; }  /* default — or set your preferred base */

h1    { font-size: 2rem;    }  /* 32px */
h2    { font-size: 1.5rem;  }  /* 24px */
p     { font-size: 1rem;    }  /* 16px */
small { font-size: 0.875rem;}  /* 14px */

/* Spacing in rem scales with user's font preference */
.section  { padding: 2rem; }   /* 32px at default size */
.card     { gap: 1.5rem; }     /* 24px at default size */
Never override the root font-size with a fixed px value — doing so disables browser font-size preferences. Set it with a percentage (font-size: 100%) or leave it at the browser default.

em is relative to the element's own font-size. When used for font-size itself, it is relative to the inherited (parent) font-size. em is useful for spacing that should scale proportionally within a component, but it compounds through nesting:
ch is the advance width of the '0' (zero) character in the current font. ex is the x-height (the height of a lowercase 'x'). ch is practical for setting readable line widths:
Viewport units are relative to the browser viewport — the visible area of the web page. They are useful for full-screen layouts and fluid scaling.
Square elements that fit any orientation
100vh can be larger than the visible area on mobile browsers where toolbars overlap the viewport
dvh solves the mobile toolbar problem — it updates as the browser UI appears and disappears
svh stays at the height with toolbars visible (conservative — nothing is clipped)
lvh stays at the height with toolbars hidden (generous — may be clipped by toolbars)
Percentages are relative to a reference value that depends on the property:
Parent element's height (parent must have explicit height)
Math Functions: calc(), min(), max(), clamp()
CSS math functions let you compute values at render time. They accept mixed units, making responsive design possible without JavaScript.
clamp(min, preferred, max) sets a value that scales fluidly between a minimum and maximum. It is the recommended tool for fluid typography:
CSS supports multiple color notations. The choice affects readability, toolability, and gamut (range of representable colors).
Most common. #rrggbb or shorthand #rgb. Optional alpha: #3b82f680
Best for accessible color scales; perceptually uniform
147 named colors; avoid in production code
oklch is the recommended modern notation for design tokens — it produces perceptually consistent color scales
hsl is still useful for component-level theming where you need to reason about lightness
currentColor is powerful for SVG icons — the icon automatically matches the surrounding text color
color-mix() (supported in 2023+) blends two colors: color-mix(in oklch, blue 40%, white)
Some CSS properties take unitless numbers. Adding a unit to these properties is either invalid or produces different behavior:
Children inherit the multiplier, not a computed px value
A ratio — unit would not make sense

Container query units (cq*) are relative to the nearest ancestor that establishes a containment context via container-type. They enable truly component-relative sizing, unlike viewport units which are always relative to the window.
1% of the container's inline size (width in LTR)
1% of the container's block size (height in LTR)
Container query units are supported in all modern browsers since 2023. They are the correct unit for component-level fluid sizing in Angular design systems.
Choosing the Right Unit — Quick Guide
Angular: Using Units in Component Styles
Angular components are encapsulated by default, so component CSS custom properties provide a clean theming API that respects the cascade:
Callers override individual tokens without needing to know the component internals:

/* Consuming stylesheet */
app-button { --btn-bg: #10b981; }  /* green brand override */
font-size in px — blocks the user's browser font-size preference; use rem instead
height in % without a parent height — percentage heights require the parent to have a known height or use flex/grid
em for font-size in deeply nested components — multiplies unexpectedly; prefer rem
vh on mobile — 100vh can extend under browser toolbars; use 100dvh instead
padding/margin % vertical values relative to height — they always use the parent's width, which surprises many developers
line-height in px — does not scale when users change font size; always use unitless or rem
Time values are used in transition-duration, transition-delay, animation-duration, and animation-delay. They accept seconds (s) or milliseconds (ms):
0.2s and 200ms are equivalent — ms is often easier to reason about for short durations
A zero-second transition still requires a unit: transition: 0s (or 0ms) — bare 0 is invalid for time
Negative animation-delay starts the animation partway through its cycle
animation-duration must be positive; transition-duration 0s means instant (no animation)
Angle values are used in transform functions (rotate, skew), gradients, and hsl() color values:
Resolution units describe how many physical pixels correspond to one CSS reference pixel. They appear in @media queries for targeting high-DPI screens:
Dots per CSS px — most useful for screen (1dppx = 96dpi)
Modern practice: use SVGs or responsive images (<picture>, srcset) instead of resolution media queries
The standard Retina threshold is 2dppx; high-end mobile screens reach 3–4dppx
image-set() is the CSS function equivalent: background: image-set(url('1x.png') 1x, url('2x.png') 2x)

A design token system centralizes all unit-based decisions as CSS custom properties in a single :root block. Every component then uses those tokens instead of hard-coded values, making global changes (brand color, spacing scale, radius) a one-line edit.
Angular 17+ recommendation: export design tokens as a TypeScript object alongside the CSS variables, so components can read them programmatically for canvas/SVG/D3 work without duplicating values:

CSS units fall into four categories: absolute (px for screen), font-relative (rem for consistent scaling, em for proportional within a component, ch for text-width sizing), viewport (vw/vh/dvh for full-screen layouts), and percentage (relative to containing block). Math functions — calc(), min(), max(), clamp() — let you mix units and create fluid, constrained values. Color values range from hex and rgb to hsl and oklch; oklch is the best choice for accessible, consistent color palettes. Container query units (cqi, cqw) enable component-relative sizing when combined with container-type. In Angular, expose component sizing through CSS custom properties to create a clean, themable API.
